It is painted on colored paper. Versions are held by the {{ill|Hie Shrine (Numazu)|ja|日枝神社 (沼津市)|lt=Numazu Hie Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hie Shrine (Numazu)|1=Hie Shrine (Numazu)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117864683}}, {{ill|Enryaku-ji|en|Enryaku-ji|WD=Q917195|lt=Enryaku-ji|1=Enryaku-ji}} on {{ill|Mount Hiei|en|Mount Hiei|WD=Q748040|lt=Mount Hiei|1=Mount Hiei}}, the {{ill|Kubosō Memorial Museum of Arts, Izumi|en|Kubosō Memorial Museum of Arts, Izumi|WD=Q11417787|lt=Kubosō Memorial Museum of Arts, Izumi|1=Kubosō Memorial Museum of Arts, Izumi}}, the {{ill|Hyōgo Prefectural Museum of Art|en|Hyōgo Prefectural Museum of Art|WD=Q3329607|lt=Hyōgo Prefectural Museum of Art|1=Hyōgo Prefectural Museum of Art}}, and the {{ill|Hosomi Museum|en|Hosomi Museum|WD=Q11606115|lt=Hosomi Museum|1=Hosomi Museum}}, each designated as {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}.<ref>Agency for Cultural Affairs, "National Treasures and Important Cultural Properties of Japan," vol.1 Painting (upper volume), Mainichi Shimbun, 1997, pp.424-427.</ref>
=="Sannō Reigenki" in "Tokitsugu Kyōki"== The {{ill|Diary|en|Diary|WD=Q185598|lt=Diary|1=Diary}} of the Sengoku-period {{ill|Kugyō|en|Kugyō|WD=Q3138036|lt=Kugyō|1=Kugyō}}, {{ill|Yamashina Tokitsugu|ja|山科言継|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Tokitsugu|1=Yamashina Tokitsugu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11470822}}, called {{ill|Tokitsugu Kyōki|ja|言継卿記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokitsugu Kyōki|1=Tokitsugu-kyō-ki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11630984}}, mentions "Sannō Engi 15 volumes," "Hiyoshi-sha Engi 15 volumes," and "Hiyoshi Reigen-e." These originals no longer exist, and all extant scrolls known as "Sannō Reigenki" are copies.<ref>Hamada Takashi, "Illustration Commentary," Shūeisha, 1980, p.131; Hosomi Museum, 2017, p.158.</ref> There are two lineages: one from Numazu Hie Shrine (Hie Shrine version), dating from the Kamakura period, and another focused on biographies of monks from Mount Hiei, dating from the Muromachi period.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/2656 |title=Sannō Reigenki|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|accessdate=2023-05-29}}</ref>
==Sannō Reigenki (Numazu Hie Shrine)== *Colored paper handscroll, Kamakura period (1288 inscription), deposited at the Tokyo National Museum. Depicts the founding legend of Hie Shrine in Numazu, focusing on Fujiwara no Moromichi's divine punishment by Hiyoshi Sannō.<ref>Ministry of Education, "Nihon Kokuhō Zenshū," vol.29, 1927.</ref>
==Sanmon Sōden== The versions held by Enryaku-ji, Kubosō Memorial Museum, Hyōgo Prefectural Museum, and Hosomi Museum narrate monks associated with Mount Hiei, thus known as '''Sanmon Sōden'''. They commonly depict distinctive blue mist outlined in white, a characteristic of Muromachi period painting.<ref>Nakano Genzō, "Shaji Engi-e Ron," 1975, p.42.</ref>
===Enryaku-ji version=== *Colored paper handscroll, Muromachi period, originally owned by Shōgen-ji. Includes stories such as prayers by Kakushu, leading to Emperor Kōgon's birth.<ref>Umedu, cited above; Komatsu, 1984, p.83.</ref>
===Kubosō Memorial Museum version=== *Two scrolls, Muromachi period, formerly Renge-ji. Includes a story of a boy raised by monkeys and later becoming a monk on Mt. Hiei.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.imes.boj.or.jp/cm/collection/tenjizuroku/mod/webP19_26MedievalTimes.pdf |title=Medieval Exhibit Catalog|publisher=Currency Museum|accessdate=2023-05-31}}</ref>
===Hyōgo Prefectural Museum version=== *One scroll, Muromachi period, former Inoue Kaoru collection, depicting monks Senka and Shōkyū guided by bees, considered messengers of Hiyoshi Sannō.<ref>Nakano, 1975, p.100.</ref>
===Hosomi Museum version=== *One scroll, Muromachi period, depicts monks traveling to China seeking sutras and miraculous intervention by Prince Evil (Third Prince of Sannō) rescuing a monk from hell.<ref>Nakano, 1975, p.101.</ref>
==Notes and References== <references />
==Bibliography== * Umedu Jirō, "Sannō Reigenki Emaki – Renge-ji and Others," 1940. * Komatsu Shigemi, "Sannō Reigenki," 1984. * Shimosaka Mamoru, "Formation and Transformation of Sannō Reigenki," 2003.
